questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: Your agent helps users debug code. After fixing a tricky asyncio deadlock, you want the agent to remember the fix pattern for future sessions. Which store is the right target?
options: A. Episodic store — log the full conversation turn as a raw event
B. Semantic store — distill the fix into a factual entry like 'asyncio deadlocks caused by nested event loops require run_until_complete replacement'
C. Procedural store — save the step-by-step debugging sequence as a reusable skill
D. No store — retrieval will surface the raw log next time anyway
questions.explanation: Procedural memory stores learned skills and repeatable action sequences. A debugging pattern is exactly that — a skill the agent should invoke directly. Episodic is for raw events, semantic is for facts; neither captures the how-to structure of a fix pattern.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: An agent has run 500 tasks over two weeks. Its episodic store has 5,000 entries. You trigger a compaction run. What is the primary risk you must guard against?
options: A. The compaction LLM call costs too many tokens
B. Rare but critical events get dropped because they scored low on recency and importance at compaction time
C. Semantic entries conflict with episodic entries and cause hallucination
D. Compaction increases retrieval latency because the store grows larger
questions.explanation: Compaction is lossy compression — the LLM summarizes clusters of events and discards originals. A rare edge-case event (a security exception, a one-off user preference) may have low frequency and low recency, so it scores low and gets dropped. This is why importance scoring at write time — before compaction — matters.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: Generative Agents compute retrieval score as: recency + relevance + importance. A memory written 6 days ago is highly relevant and marked important. A newer memory from yesterday scores low on the other two. Which ranks higher?
options: A. The newer memory — recency always dominates
B. The older memory — relevance and importance together outweigh recency decay
C. They always tie — the formula normalizes all three to equal weight
D. Neither — the agent re-generates the memory from scratch
questions.explanation: The formula sums all three normalized scores equally. Two high-scoring dimensions (relevance + importance) on the older memory can outweigh one high-scoring dimension (recency) on the newer one. Recency does not dominate — it is one equal term.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: True or False: In MemGPT's two-tier paging model, when a memory is evicted from the context window, it is permanently deleted.
options: A. True
B. False
questions.explanation: MemGPT evicts pages from context back to external storage, not deleted. The page is retrievable — the agent can issue a page-in request to load it back. The whole point is that storage is cheap and durable while context is scarce and temporary.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: A user mentions offhand: 'I prefer responses in Spanish.' The agent handles 20 more messages, none about language. At session end, a reflection pass runs. The language preference scores low on recency. What write policy prevents this preference from being lost?
options: A. Rely on the reflection pass — it will extract all facts automatically
B. Assign a high importance score at write time based on event type (user preference = high importance), not just recency or frequency
C. Store every message as a raw episodic entry so nothing is ever lost
D. Ask the user to repeat their preference at the start of each session
questions.explanation: User preferences are high-stakes but may appear only once. A write policy that tags certain event types — preferences, constraints, credentials — with a high importance score at write time ensures they survive reflection and compaction. Recency-only or frequency-only scoring will drop them.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: An agent successfully debugs a memory leak using a four-step process for the third time this week. A new session starts. Which memory type should the agent retrieve first — and why not the other two?
questions.answer: Procedural memory, because the agent has a validated, repeatable skill for this task class.
questions.explanation: Episodic memory would give raw event logs from past sessions — useful context but verbose and unstructured. Semantic memory might hold the fact 'memory leaks are caused by unreleased references' — true but not actionable. Procedural memory stores the exact workflow that succeeded, so the agent can execute it directly without replaying or re-deriving anything.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: Your agent compacted 50 episodes from a web-scraping run into a 2-paragraph summary, then deleted the originals. Three tasks later, it needs the exact URL that returned a 429 error in episode 23. What went wrong?
questions.answer: The originals were deleted after compaction, so the specific URL is gone. Archives should be kept, not deleted.
questions.explanation: Compaction is lossy. Specific details like error codes and URLs are exactly what gets lost in a summary. The safe pattern is: reflect first (extract important facts to semantic store), then compact, then archive originals — never delete them.

questions.source_title: Agent Memory Architectures: Episodic, Semantic, and Procedural Stores with Retrieval-Augmented Recall
questions.question: Memory X: 10 min ago, relevance 0.50, importance 0.1. Memory Y: 4 hours ago, relevance 0.88, importance 0.9. Weights alpha=0.3, beta=0.4, gamma=0.3. Which wins?
questions.answer: Memory Y wins with score ~0.914 vs X's ~0.530.
questions.explanation: X score: 0.3(0.999) + 0.4(0.50) + 0.3(0.1) = 0.530. Y score: 0.3(0.973) + 0.4(0.88) + 0.3(0.9) = 0.914. Even though Y is older, its much higher relevance and importance overwhelm the small recency advantage of X.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: Your circuit breaker has a minimum request window of 10 and a failure-rate threshold of 50%. In the last 8 requests, 6 failed. What does the circuit breaker do?
options: A. Opens immediately because failure rate is 75%
B. Stays closed because the window hasn't filled yet
C. Moves to half-open to probe the upstream
D. Resets the failure counter and starts over
questions.explanation: The circuit breaker requires a minimum of 10 requests before it can trip. With only 8 requests, the window is not full, so no decision is made and the breaker stays closed. The common mistake is thinking failure rate alone triggers the trip — the minimum window exists to prevent false positives on small samples. Options A and C are wrong because the threshold check only runs after the window is satisfied.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: Your FastAPI service has one shared semaphore of 100 slots covering both your primary LLM upstream and your embedding service. The LLM starts responding slowly — requests pile up holding slots for 30 seconds each. What happens to embedding requests?
options: A. Embedding requests are unaffected because they use a different model
B. Embedding requests get throttled or rejected because the shared slots are exhausted
C. The semaphore automatically prioritizes embedding requests as they are faster
D. The LLM requests time out first, freeing slots before embeddings are affected
questions.explanation: A shared semaphore means both services compete for the same pool of slots. Slow LLM requests hold slots for 30 seconds, draining the pool. Embedding requests — even though they are fast and healthy — cannot acquire slots and get rejected. This is exactly the cascade the bulkhead pattern prevents by giving each upstream its own isolated semaphore. Options A and C are wrong because semaphores have no awareness of which upstream a slot is for.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: Your circuit breaker trips open. After the reset timer expires, one request is allowed through and succeeds. What happens next?
options: A. The breaker closes fully and all traffic resumes
B. The breaker stays half-open and sends several more probes before closing
C. The breaker closes fully only after the probe succeeds and the minimum window fills again
D. The reset timer restarts and another probe is sent after the next interval
questions.explanation: In the standard circuit breaker state machine, a single successful probe in half-open is enough to close the breaker and resume normal traffic. The breaker does not wait for multiple successes or a full window — that would defeat the purpose of the fast recovery path. Options B and C add extra steps that most implementations do not require. The common mistake is confusing the half-open probe count with the closed-state window.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: True or False: For an LLM upstream, setting your read timeout to the same value as your average TTFT is a safe default because most tokens arrive after that point anyway.
options: A. True
B. False
questions.explanation: False. TTFT is the time to the first token — the read timeout must stay open for the entire decode stream, not just the first token. If you set read timeout equal to TTFT, the connection drops after the first token arrives and decoding cuts off. You need separate timeouts: a tight one for connect, a moderate one for TTFT (first byte), and a much longer one for the full decode. Conflating TTFT with total response time is a common and expensive mistake.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: Your LLM service averages 500ms TTFT and 8s total response time. You set a bulkhead semaphore of 50 slots with a 200ms acquisition timeout. Under load, requests start timing out on semaphore acquisition — but the LLM upstream is healthy. What is the most likely cause?
options: A. The circuit breaker opened and is blocking acquisitions
B. 50 slots is too small for the concurrency your traffic demands, given 8s hold time per slot
C. The acquisition timeout is too long and should be reduced to 100ms
D. The LLM is actually degraded and TTFT has silently increased
questions.explanation: By Little's Law, throughput = concurrency / latency. With 8s per request and 50 slots, max throughput is about 6 requests per second. If your traffic exceeds that, slots fill up and acquisitions time out. The fix is to increase the semaphore size or add more replicas — not to tighten the acquisition timeout. Option C makes it worse. Option A is wrong because circuit breakers do not block semaphore acquisition directly.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: The circuit breaker has been open for 30 seconds and transitions to half-open. The single probe request fails. What happens next?
questions.answer: The breaker immediately returns to open state and the reset timer starts over.
questions.explanation: Half-open allows one probe to test upstream health. A failed probe means the upstream is still unhealthy, so the breaker trips back to open rather than letting more traffic through. It won't try again until another full reset period passes.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: Your service has a single shared semaphore with limit 100. vLLM calls slow down and hold slots for 30 seconds each. At 4 requests/second hitting vLLM, roughly how long before Redis endpoints also start failing?
questions.answer: About 25 seconds (100 slots ÷ 4 req/s = 25s to exhaust the shared pool).
questions.explanation: By Little's Law, steady-state concurrent requests = arrival rate × average hold time. At 4 req/s with 30s hold time, you accumulate 4×30=120 in-flight at steady state — which exceeds the cap of 100, so you hit saturation even faster. Once the shared pool is full, all upstreams including Redis are blocked regardless of their own health.

questions.source_title: Circuit Breakers and Bulkheads: Resilience Patterns for LLM Backend Services
questions.question: You set a single 30-second timeout on your vLLM calls. A request waits 25 seconds in the prefill queue, then starts streaming normally. What happens, and what should you have done instead?
questions.answer: The request gets killed at 30 seconds even though vLLM is healthy. Use a separate read timeout (e.g., 15 seconds between chunks) to catch mid-stream hangs, and a total timeout aligned to your SLA — not a flat timeout that conflates queue wait with streaming time.
questions.explanation: TTFT and decode time are independent phases. A flat timeout treats slow queuing as a failure. The read timeout catches actual hangs mid-stream, while the total timeout enforces your SLA ceiling without penalizing slow-start requests that are otherwise healthy.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: You run pairwise eval: Response A vs B. The judge picks A 65% of the time. You flip the order and B wins 60% of the time. What should you conclude?
options: A. A is better — it still wins more than half overall.
B. B is better — it won when presented first, which is harder.
C. Position bias dominates — treat it as a near-tie and investigate.
D. The eval is broken — discard all results.
questions.explanation: When reversing order flips the winner, position bias is the dominant signal, not quality. Option A ignores that the 65% win rate disappeared when order changed. Option D is too extreme — you can debias by averaging both-order scores.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: Which judge setup is most likely to produce reliable, unbiased scores for a medical QA system?
options: A. GPT-4 generates and GPT-4 judges — same model means consistent perspective.
B. Claude generates and GPT-4 judges — different families reduce self-preference bias.
C. No rubric — rubrics constrain the model's natural reasoning.
D. Run each comparison once to save API costs.
questions.explanation: Self-preference bias means a model subtly favors its own outputs. Using a different family for judging removes that. Option A is the classic mistake. Option C produces inconsistent scores since the judge invents different criteria each time. Option D can't detect position bias.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: True or False: Verbosity bias means the judge always prefers shorter answers because they are easier to verify.
options: A. True
B. False
questions.explanation: Verbosity bias runs the opposite direction — LLMs prefer longer, more detailed responses even when length doesn't improve quality. The mitigation is explicit rubric instructions against favoring length.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: G-Eval returns: p('3')=0.4, p('4')=0.5, p('5')=0.1. What is the G-Eval score, and why is it better than argmax?
options: A. Score=4.0; argmax is fine since it picks the most likely score.
B. Score=3.7; G-Eval captures the uncertainty between 3 and 4.
C. Score=4.0; G-Eval and argmax are equivalent when one token dominates.
D. Score=3.5; take the median.
questions.explanation: G-Eval score = 3×0.4 + 4×0.5 + 5×0.1 = 3.7. The argmax gives 4, discarding the 40% probability mass on score 3. For ranking two responses that both get argmax=4, G-Eval can still differentiate them by exact weighted score.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: Which rubric for an LLM judge is better?
options: A. 'Quality: 1=bad, 5=good'
B. 'Accuracy: 5=all facts verifiable, 3=one minor error, 1=multiple major errors'
C. 'Helpfulness, tone, clarity' — three broad dimensions without scales.
D. No rubric — let the model reason freely.
questions.explanation: Concrete level anchors eliminate the model's need to interpret what 'good' means — it just matches the response to a specific description. Option A's abstract scale produces high variance. Option C has no scale so scores aren't comparable across calls. Option D is the worst: the judge invents different criteria every time.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: You have 50 model responses to rank. Why might pairwise scoring give more reliable rankings than pointwise?
questions.answer: Pairwise is a relative comparison — LLMs are more consistent at 'which is better' than at assigning an absolute score on a scale.
questions.explanation: Absolute scale scores drift between prompts. Relative rankings are anchored to a specific pair, which reduces variance.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: Your eval shows Response A winning 70% of comparisons. You flip the order and A wins only 35%. What does this tell you?
questions.answer: Strong position bias — the judge is mostly picking whichever response appears first.
questions.explanation: A reliable judge gives similar win rates regardless of order. A flip this large means merit isn't the signal.

questions.source_title: LLM-as-Judge: Building Reliable Eval Pipelines
questions.question: Why does G-Eval compute a weighted average of score token probabilities instead of taking the highest-probability score?
questions.answer: The weighted average captures uncertainty — if the model is 60% on 4 and 40% on 3, the score is 3.6, not 4.
questions.explanation: Hard argmax throws away confidence information. The weighted score reflects graded uncertainty and ranks responses more reliably.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: Mixtral 8x7B has 47B total parameters but only ~12-13B active per token. You're budgeting GPU memory for serving. Which statement is correct?
options: A. You only need enough VRAM to hold 12-13B parameters because inactive experts are offloaded at runtime.
B. You need VRAM for all 47B parameters, but FLOPs per token are equivalent to a ~12-13B dense model.
C. You need VRAM for all 47B parameters and FLOPs per token match a 47B dense model.
D. You only load the top-2 experts per layer into VRAM; the rest stay on CPU.
questions.explanation: All expert weights must live in VRAM simultaneously because the router decides which experts to use at runtime — you can't know in advance. The FLOPs saving is real: only top-K expert FFNs execute per token, so compute cost tracks active params (~12-13B), not total params. Option A and D describe offloading schemes that are not standard and add latency. Option C is wrong because the whole point of MoE is that FLOPs scale with active params.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: You train a 16-expert MoE. After 10k steps you check expert utilization. Expert 0 handles 61% of tokens, Expert 1 handles 22%, and the remaining 14 experts share 17%. Your aux loss weight alpha is 0.001. What is the most likely cause and the right fix?
options: A. The capacity factor is too low, causing token dropping that skews the utilization stats.
B. Alpha is too small — the load-balancing loss is too weak to prevent router collapse. Increase alpha to ~0.01.
C. Top-K is set to 1. Switch to top-2 routing to force token distribution.
D. The z-loss is missing. Add z-loss and the imbalance will self-correct without changing alpha.
questions.explanation: The utilization pattern (one expert at 61%) is classic router collapse. The load-balancing loss L_aux is scaled by alpha; at 0.001 it is 10x weaker than the recommended 0.01 default, so the router ignores it. Raising alpha to 0.01 strengthens the gradient signal that pushes f_i * P_i toward uniform. Option A is wrong — capacity factor affects dropping, not the underlying routing preference. Option C might help marginally but does not fix the root cause. Option D: z-loss penalizes large logit magnitudes but does not directly enforce load balance the way L_aux does.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: In a top-2 MoE router, after selecting the two highest-scoring experts, the output is computed as: output = g1 * E1(x) + g2 * E2(x). Where do g1 and g2 come from?
options: A. The raw softmax probabilities for expert 1 and expert 2.
B. The raw logits for expert 1 and expert 2, passed through a sigmoid.
C. The softmax probabilities for expert 1 and expert 2, renormalized so g1 + g2 = 1.
D. Fixed weights of 0.5 each, so both experts contribute equally.
questions.explanation: After selecting top-2, you renormalize: g_i = softmax_score_i / (softmax_score_1 + softmax_score_2). This ensures the gating weights sum to 1 and the output scale is consistent regardless of how probability mass was distributed across all N experts before selection. Using raw softmax scores (Option A) would make the output scale depend on how many experts exist. Raw logits (Option B) are unbounded. Fixed 0.5/0.5 weights (Option D) would ignore router confidence entirely.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: You have a batch of 512 tokens, 8 experts, top-2 routing, and capacity_factor=1.25. An expert receives 110 tokens. What happens to the overflow tokens, and how many tokens can each expert process?
options: A. Capacity = 80 tokens per expert. The 30 overflow tokens are re-routed to their third-choice expert.
B. Capacity = 80 tokens per expert. The 30 overflow tokens are dropped — they skip expert computation and pass through as the residual stream unchanged.
C. Capacity = 100 tokens per expert. The 10 overflow tokens are queued and processed in the next forward pass.
D. Capacity = 80 tokens per expert. Overflow tokens get the average output of all experts.
questions.explanation: Capacity = 1.25 × (512 / 8) = 80 tokens per expert. Tokens beyond that limit are dropped: they bypass the expert and the residual connection carries their representation forward unchanged. There is no re-routing mechanism in standard top-K routing. Queuing across forward passes breaks the stateless per-token computation model. Averaging expert outputs would require running all experts anyway.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: Expert-choice routing (where each expert picks its top-K tokens instead of each token picking its top-K experts) eliminates token dropping and always produces better load balance than token-choice routing.
options: A. True — experts pick exactly K tokens each, so load is perfectly balanced and no token is ever dropped.
B. False — expert-choice routing does balance load perfectly, but it cannot be used for autoregressive decoding because a token cannot wait for all experts to vote on whether to include it.
questions.explanation: Expert-choice routing eliminates dropping and guarantees perfect load balance — that part is true. But in autoregressive inference you generate one token at a time. That token needs to know immediately which expert(s) will process it; it cannot wait for experts to collectively rank a batch and choose it. This makes expert-choice routing incompatible with standard decode-time inference, which is why Mixtral and most production models use token-choice top-K.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: Mixtral 8x7B has roughly 47B total parameters but only ~12-13B active per token. What determines the active parameter count — and why does it match a 13B dense model's compute cost rather than a 47B model's?
questions.answer: Active parameter count is determined by top-K routing: only 2 of 8 experts fire per token. Each expert is ~7B-scale FFN weights, so 2 experts ≈ 12-13B active. FLOPs scale with active params (the weights actually multiplied), not total params sitting in memory — so inference compute matches a ~13B dense model.
questions.explanation: The key insight is that FLOPs per token are proportional to the parameters actually used in computation, not the total model size. The other 6 experts occupy memory but contribute zero FLOPs for tokens that don't route to them.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: A token gets router logits [3.0, 1.0, 2.5, 0.2] for 4 experts. After softmax and top-2 selection, which two experts are chosen and how are their outputs combined?
questions.answer: Experts 1 and 3 (highest logits: 3.0 and 2.5). Their outputs are combined as a weighted sum using their renormalized softmax probabilities — Expert 1 gets more weight since its logit was higher.
questions.explanation: Softmax preserves the ordering of logits, so the top-2 by logit are always the top-2 by softmax probability. The final output is w1*E1_out + w3*E3_out, where w1 and w3 are the softmax probs renormalized to sum to 1 over just those two experts.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: You have a batch of 1024 tokens, 16 experts, and capacity_factor=1.5. Expert 7 receives 130 token assignments. How many tokens does expert 7 actually process, and how many are dropped?
questions.answer: Capacity = 1.5 × (1024 / 16) = 96. Expert 7 processes 96 tokens and drops 34.
questions.explanation: The capacity formula gives the max tokens any one expert can handle. Anything beyond that cap is dropped — passed through the layer without being processed by the expert.

questions.source_title: Mixture of Experts: Architecture, Sparse Routing, and Production Tradeoffs
questions.question: In the Switch Transformer auxiliary loss L_aux = alpha * N * sum(f_i * P_i), why is the product f_i * P_i used instead of just f_i^2?
questions.answer: f_i is computed via argmax (hard routing) and has no gradient. P_i is the soft router probability and is differentiable. The product lets gradients flow through P_i to push the router toward balance, while f_i provides the actual load signal.
questions.explanation: If you used f_i^2 alone, there would be no gradient path back to the router weights. P_i is the differentiable proxy — it measures the router's soft intent for each expert, and its gradient tells the router to raise probabilities for underloaded experts.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: A ReAct agent runs 5 steps to answer a user query. A Plan-Execute agent solves the same query with a 5-step plan. How many LLM calls does each make?
options: A. ReAct: 1 call, Plan-Execute: 5 calls
B. ReAct: 5 calls, Plan-Execute: 1 call
C. ReAct: 5 calls, Plan-Execute: 2 calls (plan + possible replan)
D. Both make 5 calls
questions.explanation: Plan-Execute uses 1 LLM call to generate the plan, then executes each step without any LLM calls in between — so the total is at minimum 1 call (just plan, happy path) up to 2+ if replanning kicks in. ReAct calls the LLM before every action, so 5 steps = 5 calls. Option B is wrong because Plan-Execute still needs at least 1 call to produce the plan. Option D conflates the two architectures entirely.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: Your Plan-Execute agent has a 4-step plan: fetch user data → enrich with CRM → write to DB → send email. Step 2 (CRM API) returns an empty response due to a rate limit. What happens next?
options: A. The agent retries step 2 automatically because it observes the empty result
B. The agent continues to step 3 and writes empty/corrupt data to the DB
C. The plan is discarded and a new plan is generated from scratch
D. Execution halts with a clear error message to the user
questions.explanation: In a basic Plan-Execute setup there is no LLM between steps, so no reasoning catches the bad output. Execution continues blindly and step 3 receives whatever step 2 produced — in this case, nothing. Option A is how ReAct would behave (it observes the result and can reason about it). Option C only happens if you have an explicit reflection checkpoint wired up. Option D assumes error handling exists that was never built.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: You are building an agent that browses the web to answer research questions. Pages load unpredictably — some 404, some return paywalled content, some require a follow-up click. Which architecture fits best?
options: A. Plan-Execute — generate a full research plan upfront and execute it fast
B. ReAct — reason after every tool result so the agent can adapt to each page
C. Plan-Execute with a reflection checkpoint at the end only
D. Plan-Execute — because web browsing steps are always reliable
questions.explanation: Unpredictable tools are exactly the case where ReAct wins. Each observation (404, paywall, partial content) feeds back into the next Think step and the agent can change course. Plan-Execute would march through a stale plan even as every tool call falls apart. A single end-of-run reflection checkpoint (option C) is too late — the bad outputs have already propagated.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: True or False: ReAct agents get reflection for free because the LLM reads the full trajectory before every action.
options: A. True
B. False
questions.explanation: True. In ReAct the full Think→Act→Observe history is appended to the context before each new LLM call. The model literally reads what happened, which means it can catch failures, contradict itself, or change strategy without any extra engineering. Plan-Execute skips this per-step reasoning by design, so you have to add reflection checkpoints manually if you want the same capability.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: A teammate proposes adding an explicit 'replanning call' to your Plan-Execute agent whenever a step returns an error. What problem does this solve and what is the trade-off?
options: A. It converts Plan-Execute into pure ReAct, removing all latency benefits
B. It adds error recovery without giving up the fast happy-path; adds latency only on failure paths
C. It has no effect — Plan-Execute can never recover from errors
D. It reduces latency by catching errors early before expensive steps run
questions.explanation: A replanning call is a reflection checkpoint: on success the agent still runs 1 plan call + N cheap execution steps. On failure, one extra LLM call kicks in to replan. This is the recommended pattern for long tasks with unreliable tools. Option A is wrong — you only call the LLM on error, not after every step like ReAct. Option D has the logic backwards; the extra call adds latency rather than removing it.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: In a ReAct agent, a tool returns an empty result. What happens next?
questions.answer: The empty result becomes the observation for the next Think step, and the LLM reasons about how to recover — such as retrying with a different query.
questions.explanation: There is no special error handler. The failure is just data appended to context. The same Think step that processes successful results also processes failures, letting the agent self-correct naturally.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: In a Plan-Execute agent, the API returns 0 rows in step 1. What happens to steps 2 through 5?
questions.answer: They execute anyway, using the empty result as input.
questions.explanation: Plan-Execute has no replanning mechanism mid-execution. Each step runs regardless of what the previous step returned. An empty result is passed forward silently, often producing no errors but also no useful output.

questions.source_title: ReAct vs Plan-Execute Agents: Reflection Loops, Error Recovery, and When Each Wins
questions.question: In a Plan-Execute agent, why does error recovery require extra code — while in ReAct it happens automatically?
questions.answer: Because Plan-Execute runs execution steps without LLM calls, so there is no LLM present to reason about failures. ReAct includes an LLM Think step after every tool result, so it reads the error and reasons around it naturally.
questions.explanation: ReAct's loop structure means the LLM always sees the last tool output before acting again. Plan-Execute skips the LLM during execution for speed — but that means you must explicitly route failures to a replanning LLM call if you want recovery.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: A draft model proposes the token 'return' with q=0.9. The verifier assigns p=0.3 to that token. You roll a uniform random number u=0.25. Do you accept the token?
options: A. Yes — u=0.25 is less than p=0.3, so accept.
B. Yes — u=0.25 is less than min(1, p/q)=0.33, so accept.
C. No — p < q, so always reject when the verifier disagrees.
D. No — u=0.25 is less than q=0.9 but the verifier is skeptical, so reject.
questions.explanation: The acceptance criterion is u < min(1, p/q). Here min(1, 0.3/0.9) = 0.33, and u=0.25 < 0.33, so the token is accepted. Option A compares u to p directly — wrong formula. Option C is wrong because p < q does not guarantee rejection; it only lowers the acceptance probability. The common mistake is forgetting to divide p by q before comparing to u.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: The verifier rejects a draft token at position 3 in a 5-token draft sequence. The corrected distribution gives a new token. What happens to draft tokens at positions 4 and 5?
options: A. They are verified independently and may still be accepted.
B. They are discarded — all tokens after the rejection position are thrown away.
C. They are re-scored by the draft model before the next step.
D. Position 4 is discarded but position 5 is kept if it matches the verifier's top-1.
questions.explanation: When a rejection happens at position k, tokens at k+1 and beyond are discarded. The verifier samples a corrected token at position k from normalize(max(0, p-q)) and generation continues from there. Options A and D are wrong — verification is not independent per token once a rejection occurs. The common mistake is thinking the verifier can partially salvage later tokens.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: You have alpha=0.8 (acceptance rate) and gamma=4 (draft tokens). What is the expected number of tokens accepted per verifier call, using E[accepted] = (1 - alpha^(gamma+1)) / (1 - alpha)?
questions.explanation: E[accepted] = (1 - 0.8^5) / (1 - 0.8) = (1 - 0.32768) / 0.2 = 0.67232 / 0.2 = 3.36. Option A (3.2) is just alpha * gamma — wrong formula. Option C assumes all drafts are accepted. The formula accounts for the geometric falloff: each successive token is accepted only if all previous ones were, so expected length is a geometric series sum.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: EAGLE-2 outperforms Medusa (3.5-4.5 vs 2.5-3.5 accepted tokens). What is the primary architectural reason?
options: A. EAGLE-2 uses more draft heads, so it explores more candidate tokens.
B. EAGLE-2 drafts at the hidden state level and builds dynamic trees ranked by joint probability, not fixed speculative heads.
C. EAGLE-2 skips tree attention masking, so verification is faster.
D. Medusa uses rejection sampling; EAGLE-2 uses greedy acceptance which inflates its numbers.
questions.explanation: EAGLE's draft model predicts h_{t+1} from h_t and the token embedding e_t — it works in hidden state space and builds draft trees dynamically, ranked by joint probability. Medusa uses fixed speculative heads that don't adapt to context. Option A conflates head count with draft quality. Option C is wrong — EAGLE-2 does use tree attention masking. Option D is wrong — both use the same rejection sampling criterion.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: You deploy speculative decoding in vLLM to speed up your LLM serving endpoint. Traffic grows and average batch size hits 32. A colleague says you should disable speculative decoding. Are they right, and why?
options: A. Wrong — larger batches mean more tokens accepted per step, improving throughput further.
B. Right — at batch size 32, the verifier is already compute-bound, so running the draft model adds overhead with little gain.
C. Wrong — vLLM automatically scales draft length gamma to compensate for large batches.
D. Right — the draft model can't handle batches larger than 16, causing OOM errors.
questions.explanation: Speculative decoding's speedup comes from amortizing verifier compute across accepted tokens. At large batch sizes (>8-16), the verifier forward pass is already memory-bandwidth saturated — adding draft model cost on top hurts, not helps. SGLang auto-disables speculative decoding in this regime; vLLM does not, so you must disable it manually. Option A is backwards — batching hurts, not helps. Option C is incorrect — neither vLLM nor SGLang dynamically scales gamma based on batch size.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: The draft model proposes the token 'run' with q('run') = 0.4. The verifier assigns p('run') = 0.2. What is the acceptance probability, and what happens if the token is rejected?
questions.answer: Acceptance probability is min(1, 0.2/0.4) = 0.5. If rejected, a new token is resampled from the corrected distribution normalize(max(0, p(x) - q(x))), and all subsequent draft tokens are discarded.
questions.explanation: The draft model was overconfident about 'run' compared to the verifier, so you only accept it half the time. On rejection, you don't just take the verifier's top token — you sample from the corrected distribution to maintain the exact output distribution of the verifier. Discarding all later draft tokens is essential because they were conditioned on 'run' being accepted.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: You're comparing EAGLE and Medusa on a prompt where the model is highly confident for the first 3 tokens, then very uncertain on token 4. Which system handles this better and why?
questions.answer: EAGLE-2 handles it better. It builds a dynamic tree that goes deep (few wide branches) during the confident stretch and then spreads wider at the uncertain token. Medusa uses the same fixed tree topology regardless, so it wastes draft budget with a shallow spread during the confident section and can't adapt at token 4.
questions.explanation: The key insight is that Medusa's tree shape is fixed at training time — it doesn't know the model's current confidence. EAGLE-2 reads the draft model's probability scores at runtime and allocates the tree budget dynamically. A common mistake is thinking Medusa and EAGLE-2 differ only in draft model size — the real difference is static vs dynamic tree construction.

questions.source_title: Speculative Decoding: EAGLE-2 Draft Trees and Acceptance Math
questions.question: You're running EAGLE-2 with alpha=0.8 and gamma=3. Your draft model costs about 10% of one verifier call (c=0.1). What is the approximate walltime speedup, and would you expect this to hold at a batch size of 32?
questions.answer: Speedup ≈ 2.27x at small batch sizes, but it will not hold at batch size 32.
questions.explanation: E[accepted] = (1 - 0.8^4) / (1 - 0.8) = (1 - 0.4096) / 0.2 = 2.95. Speedup = 2.95 / (1 + 3*0.1) = 2.95 / 1.3 ≈ 2.27x. At batch_size=32 the verifier is already running for 32 sequences simultaneously — it's compute-bound and each verifier call is already amortized. The draft model still adds cost (memory, compute) but no longer saves verifier calls in any meaningful sense, so the speedup collapses to near 1x or below.
